test_cases.purpose: Sample test 1: basic case with a non-trivial window [3,1,2] of length 3.
Sample test 2: large elements block long windows. Best is [1,1] at end.
Sample test 3: all elements fit within S. Answer is N.
Moderate case. Window [3,1,4,2] has sum 10>9 but [1,4,2,1]=8≤9, length 4.
Single element exceeds S. Answer is 0.
Single element within S. Answer is 1.
S very large relative to sum. All elements fit. Answer is N.
S smaller than all elements (min element is 3 > S=2). No single lantern fits. Answer is 0.
Each element equals S exactly. Only one element fits at a time.

Breaks wrong approach #2 (greedy from left). Starting from left: [1,1,1]=3 (sum 3), adding 100 exceeds 10, stop with length 3. But the correct answer is also 3 from [1,1,1] at either side. The greedy-from-left happens to get 3 here, but a case like [2,3,1,1,1] with S=4 would give 1 from left vs 3 correct.

Breaks wrong approach #1 (sort and pick smallest). Sorting gives [1,1,5,5,5], pick 2 smallest: sum=2≤2, count=2. But the two 1s are at positions 2 and 4, not consecutive. Correct answer is 1: no two consecutive elements have sum ≤ 2.
Alternating pattern. Windows of length 3: [1,2,1]=4≤5, [2,1,2]=5≤5. Length 4: [1,2,1,2]=6>5, [2,1,2,1]=6>5. Answer is 3.
Large element at end acts as barrier. Window of first 8 ones has sum 8 ≤ 10, length 8. Including 10⁹ makes sum > 10 no matter what. Tests that window correctly stops at the barrier.
All elements at max value 10⁹, S = 3×10⁹. Exactly 3 elements fit. Tests 64-bit sum handling.
Single element at 10⁹, S = 2×10⁹. Element fits. Tests large S boundary.
All elements at max value 10⁹, S = 10⁹. Only one element fits at a time.

stress_test_config.comparison: Run brute_force_solution (enumerate all O(N²) subarrays with prefix sums) and reference solution (sliding window) on each random input. Compare outputs. Any mismatch indicates a bug. Generate random N in [1,50], random S in [1,500], random P[i] in [1,100].

edge_cases_covered: S very large, all elements fit (answer N)
Large element in middle splitting the array
Boundary values with S = 3×10⁹ and elements at 10⁹
wrong_approaches_tested: Wrong approach #1 (sort and pick smallest): tested by adv_2 — P=[5,1,5,1,5], S=2. Sorting gives count 2, correct answer is 1.

Wrong approach #2 (greedy from left): tested by adv_1 — P=[1,1,1,100,1,1,1], S=10. Greedy from left gets 3, correct is also 3 but from the right side. The adversarial structure tests that the window correctly handles the barrier.
Wrong approach #3 (fixed window size): tested by adv_3 — P=[1,2,1,2,1,2,1,2], S=5. Fixed size ⌊5/1⌋=5 gives windows all exceeding 5, but correct answer is 3.
